texto: It is extraordinary that nobody nowadays under the stress of great troubles is turned into stone or a bird or a tree or some inanimate object; they used to undergo such metamorphoses in ancient times (or so they say), though whether that is myth or a true story I know not. Maybe it would be better to change one's nature into something that lacks all feeling, rather than be so sensitive to evil. Had that been possible, these calamities would in all probability have turned me to stone.
explicacao: Citação de Anna Comnena sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “It is extraordinary that nobody nowadays under the stress of great troubles is turned into stone …” articula amor, grief, metamorphosis em registro de amor, com relevância para contextos como luto e mudanca.

frase_original: It is extraordinary that nobody nowadays under the stress of great troubles is turned into stone or a bird or a tree or some inanimate object; they used to undergo such metamorphoses in ancient times (or so they say), though whether that is myth or a true story I know not. Maybe it would be better to change one's nature into something that lacks all feeling, rather than be so sensitive to evil. Had that been possible, these calamities would in all probability have turned me to stone.

texto: Even now I cannot believe that I am still alive and writing this account of the emperor's death. I put my hands to my eyes, wondering if what I am relating here is not all a dream - or maybe it is not a dream: perhaps it is a delusion and I am mad, the victim of some extraordinary and monstrous hallucination. How comes it that when he is dead I am still numbered among the living?
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Anna Comnena que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “Even now I cannot believe that I am still alive and writing this account of the emperor's death. …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre vida, grief, reflexao, com foco em luto e reflexao.
